quizQuestion: What was the first message ever sent over the ARPANET on October 29, 1969 — before the receiving computer crashed?
explanation: UCLA student programmer Charley Kline was attempting to type "login" to connect to Stanford Research Institute. He typed "l" and "o" before the SRI host crashed, making "lo" the accidental first message on the network that would become the internet.
visualDescription: A 1960s computer terminal in a dim university lab, a student hunched over a keyboard, a blinking cursor on a green-phosphor screen

quizQuestion: In what year was the first message sent over the ARPANET, marking the birth of the internet?
explanation: On October 29, 1969, the first host-to-host message was sent over ARPANET, connecting UCLA and Stanford Research Institute. The network that would eventually evolve into the internet had been born.
visualDescription: A vintage 1960s mainframe room with tape reels spinning and blinking lights, two universities connected by a glowing line on a map

quizQuestion: Who invented email in 1971 and chose the @ symbol to separate username from host — a convention we still use today?
explanation: Ray Tomlinson, an American computer programmer, sent the first network email on the ARPANET in 1971. He selected the @ symbol because it appeared on keyboards and would never appear in a person's name, creating the universal address format still used today.
visualDescription: A programmer at a 1970s teletype terminal, a stylized @ symbol floating above the machine in neon glow

quizQuestion: In what year did Ray Tomlinson send the first network email over ARPANET?
explanation: Ray Tomlinson sent the first email between two ARPANET computers in 1971. He later admitted he couldn't remember what the first message said — it was likely a test string like 'QWERTYUIOP'.
visualDescription: An early ARPANET terminal with a blinking cursor, the @ symbol glowing on screen, connected nodes visualized as dots on a network diagram

quizQuestion: Who designed the Domain Name System (DNS) in 1983, giving us the ability to use names like 'example.com' instead of raw IP addresses?
explanation: Paul Mockapetris designed the Domain Name System and published it in November 1983 as RFCs 882 and 883. DNS translates human-readable domain names into the numerical IP addresses computers use to find each other.
visualDescription: A hierarchical tree diagram of domain names branching from a root node, glowing on a blueprint-style dark background

quizQuestion: What was the very first .com domain name ever registered, on March 15, 1985?
explanation: symbolics.com, registered by computer manufacturer Symbolics Inc. on March 15, 1985, was the first .com domain name in the world. The domain still exists today as a historical landmark, purchased by a domain investor in 2009.
visualDescription: A vintage 1985 computer screen showing a blinking cursor next to 'symbolics.com', surrounded by the glow of early-era monitor phosphors

quizQuestion: Who invented the World Wide Web in 1989 while working at CERN, and also created HTML, HTTP, and URLs?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee, a British computer scientist at CERN, proposed the World Wide Web in 1989 as a system for sharing information using hypertext. He invented all three core technologies: HTML (the document format), HTTP (the transfer protocol), and URLs (the addressing system).
visualDescription: A British scientist at a NeXT workstation in a Swiss research lab, a handwritten proposal reading 'Information Management: A Proposal' on the desk

quizQuestion: In what year did Tim Berners-Lee write his proposal for the World Wide Web at CERN?
explanation: Tim Berners-Lee wrote 'Information Management: A Proposal' at CERN in 1989. His supervisor noted on it: 'Vague but exciting.' Three years later, in 1991, the first website went live.
visualDescription: A paper document titled 'Information Management: A Proposal' with a handwritten note, resting on a desk at CERN with particle physics diagrams nearby

quizQuestion: What was the URL of the world's first website, created by Tim Berners-Lee at CERN and launched in 1991?
explanation: The world's first website at info.cern.ch went live in 1991. It described the World Wide Web project and explained how to use hypertext. The page is still accessible today at its original address, preserved by CERN as a historical artifact.
visualDescription: An early 1990s web browser showing a simple text-heavy page at info.cern.ch, with blue hyperlinks on a white background

quizQuestion: What 1993 browser was the first to display images inline with text on web pages, making the web visual and helping it go mainstream?
explanation: NCSA Mosaic, released January 23, 1993, was the first browser to show images alongside text rather than in separate windows. Created by Marc Andreessen and Eric Bina at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications (NCSA), it made the web accessible to non-technical users.
visualDescription: A 1993 computer screen showing a colorful web page with inline photos, a browser window titled 'NCSA Mosaic', a university computing center in the background

quizQuestion: Who co-created the Mosaic web browser in 1993 and went on to co-found Netscape Communications?
explanation: Marc Andreessen co-created NCSA Mosaic with Eric Bina at the National Center for Supercomputing Applications while a student at the University of Illinois. He later co-founded Netscape and is now a prominent venture capitalist at Andreessen Horowitz.
visualDescription: A young programmer in a 1990s university computing lab, stacks of computer manuals nearby, a web browser prototype on the screen

quizQuestion: In what year was Netscape Navigator released, quickly becoming the dominant web browser of the 1990s?
explanation: Netscape Navigator was first released on December 15, 1994. At its peak, Netscape held more than 90% of the browser market. The 'browser wars' with Microsoft's Internet Explorer began shortly after, and the competition drove rapid advances in web technology.
visualDescription: A mid-1990s home computer showing the Netscape Navigator interface with its ship's wheel logo, an excited user browsing colorful early web pages

quizQuestion: With which Microsoft operating system was Internet Explorer first bundled in 1995, triggering the browser wars?
explanation: Internet Explorer was released on August 24, 1995, initially bundled with the Windows 95 Plus! add-on pack. Microsoft's decision to bundle IE directly with the Windows operating system was a central issue in the US vs. Microsoft antitrust case.
visualDescription: A 1995 PC box for Windows 95, an 'e' Internet Explorer icon visible on the Start menu on screen, a crowded electronics store in the background

quizQuestion: Who popularized the term 'Web 2.0' around 2004, referring to the new era of user-generated content and participatory websites?
explanation: Tim O'Reilly, founder of O'Reilly Media, popularized the concept of 'Web 2.0' around 2004, most notably through the O'Reilly Media Web 2.0 Conference. The term described a shift toward sites like Wikipedia, YouTube, and social networks where users created the content.
visualDescription: A 2004 tech conference stage with a presenter gesturing at a slide reading 'Web 2.0', surrounded by eager early-2000s laptop users in the audience

quizQuestion: Who created Archie in 1990 — considered the world's first internet search engine — while a postgraduate student at McGill University?
explanation: Alan Emtage, a student from Barbados studying at McGill University in Montreal, created Archie in 1990 to index FTP archives so users could find specific files. Its name was simply 'archive' shortened to 'Archie' — not named after the comic book character.
visualDescription: A 1990 university terminal showing a text-based search interface labeled 'Archie', a student peering at the green-screen results list

quizQuestion: Which two Stanford PhD students founded Yahoo in January 1994, initially calling it 'Jerry and David's Guide to the World Wide Web'?
explanation: Jerry Yang and David Filo started Yahoo as a personal directory of websites they found interesting. The name Yahoo stood for 'Yet Another Hierarchically Organized Oracle' — though Yang and Filo claimed to like the word because it meant 'rude, unsophisticated' in Gulliver's Travels.
visualDescription: Two graduate students at Stanford in a cramped trailer office, monitors showing a web directory with hand-curated site links

quizQuestion: AltaVista, one of the most popular early search engines launched in 1995, was developed at which now-defunct computer company?
explanation: AltaVista was developed at Digital Equipment Corporation (DEC) and launched December 15, 1995. It was famous for being among the first engines to index the full text of web pages. DEC was acquired by Compaq in 1998, and AltaVista was eventually bought by Yahoo in 2003.
visualDescription: A 1995 workstation screen showing the AltaVista search interface — a bold logo, a search box, with early '90s corporate office surroundings

quizQuestion: Which two Stanford PhD students incorporated Google on September 4, 1998, introducing the PageRank algorithm?
explanation: Larry Page and Sergey Brin met as Stanford PhD students and developed Google's PageRank algorithm, which ranked pages by the number and quality of links pointing to them. They initially tried to sell the technology for $1 million but were rejected, so they built Google into the world's most-used search engine.
visualDescription: Two graduate students in a Palo Alto garage, a whiteboard covered in math behind them, a monitor showing early Google search results

quizQuestion: What was the name of the link-analysis algorithm developed by Larry Page that powered Google's early search superiority?
explanation: PageRank (named after Larry Page, not 'page rank') ranked web pages by counting the number and quality of links pointing to them — the idea being that more important pages receive more links. This approach produced dramatically better search results than keyword-matching competitors.
visualDescription: A network graph with nodes of varying sizes representing web pages, arrows showing link connections, larger nodes glowing brighter

quizQuestion: In what year was Wikipedia founded by Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger, launching the world's largest collaboratively written encyclopedia?
explanation: Wikipedia was founded on January 15, 2001, by Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger. Starting with just a handful of articles, it grew to become the largest reference work in human history, with over 60 million articles across hundreds of languages maintained by volunteer editors.
visualDescription: A puzzle-globe logo assembling itself from interlocking pieces in various scripts, floating above a glowing laptop screen

quizQuestion: Who co-founded Wikipedia in 2001 — the free online encyclopedia that anyone can edit?
explanation: Jimmy Wales and Larry Sanger co-founded Wikipedia on January 15, 2001. Sanger coined the name (combining 'wiki' — Hawaiian for 'quick' — and 'encyclopedia'). Wales and Sanger later had a dispute over credit for the founding and both have claimed primary credit.
visualDescription: Two collaborators at a computer in an early 2000s home office, a mostly blank wiki page on screen, one of them typing the first article

quizQuestion: Which Harvard student founded Facebook in 2004, initially restricting it to Harvard before opening it to universities worldwide?
explanation: Mark Zuckerberg founded Facebook in February 2004 with his Harvard roommates Eduardo Saverin, Andrew McCollum, Dustin Moskovitz, and Chris Hughes. It launched as 'TheFacebook.com' for Harvard students before rapidly expanding. Zuckerberg dropped out of Harvard to run the company.
visualDescription: A young man in a Harvard dorm room at a laptop, a social network profile visible on screen, late-night energy drinks on the desk

quizQuestion: Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim — the three co-founders of YouTube — all previously worked together at what company?
explanation: All three YouTube founders were former employees of PayPal, which had recently been acquired by eBay. They founded YouTube on Valentine's Day 2005. Many PayPal alumni went on to found major Silicon Valley companies — they were dubbed the 'PayPal Mafia.'
visualDescription: Three young engineers in a San Bruno startup office, whiteboards full of video player sketches, early YouTube logo mockups pinned to the wall

quizQuestion: Just 19 months after YouTube launched, which company acquired it in October 2006 for $1.65 billion?
explanation: Google acquired YouTube in October 2006 for $1.65 billion in stock, one of the largest internet acquisitions at the time. The deal gave Google a dominant position in online video that persists to this day. YouTube is now estimated to be worth over $300 billion.
visualDescription: Google and YouTube logos facing each other over a handshake, stacks of stock certificates in the background

quizQuestion: Who sent the very first tweet on March 21, 2006, posting 'just setting up my twttr' on the platform he co-founded?
explanation: Jack Dorsey sent the first tweet on March 21, 2006, which read 'just setting up my twttr' — notice the original name had no vowels. Dorsey co-founded Twitter with Biz Stone, Noah Glass, and Evan Williams. He later co-founded Square (now Block) and returned to Twitter as CEO.
visualDescription: A 2006 flip-phone and early desktop computer side by side, a tweet reading 'just setting up my twttr' glowing on both screens

quizQuestion: Which two University of Virginia roommates founded Reddit in 2005, creating the platform known as 'the front page of the internet'?
explanation: Steve Huffman and Alexis Ohanian founded Reddit in 2005 after attending a talk by Y Combinator founder Paul Graham. Reddit's upvote/downvote system and 'subreddit' communities became highly influential in internet culture.
visualDescription: Two college roommates at a dorm desk with a laptop showing an early Reddit prototype, forum threads and upvote arrows sketched on a whiteboard

quizQuestion: Who created Instagram, the photo-sharing app released in October 2010 that was acquired by Facebook for ~$1 billion just 18 months later?
explanation: Kevin Systrom and Mike Krieger created Instagram, which launched on October 12, 2010. Within 24 hours it had 25,000 users. In April 2012 Facebook acquired it for approximately $1 billion — widely considered one of the best acquisitions in tech history given Instagram's later valuation of over $100 billion.
visualDescription: A 2010 iPhone screen showing the early Instagram app with a polaroid-style frame around a coffee photo, a startup office in San Francisco in the background

quizQuestion: When Jeff Bezos founded Amazon in 1994, what single product category did it initially sell before becoming 'The Everything Store'?
explanation: Jeff Bezos founded Amazon as an online bookstore because books had a massive catalog (millions of titles existed) and could be easily shipped. He chose the name 'Amazon' after the world's largest river to suggest scale. The company only began selling other products in 1998.
visualDescription: A 1995 home computer screen showing an early Amazon website listing book titles, stacks of books in a suburban garage warehouse in the background

quizQuestion: Who founded eBay in September 1995, pioneering the online auction model with an early listing for a broken laser pointer?
explanation: Pierre Omidyar founded eBay as 'AuctionWeb' in San Jose, California in September 1995. Legend has it the first item sold was a broken laser pointer for $14.83. Omidyar contacted the buyer to confirm they understood it was broken — the buyer confirmed they collected broken laser pointers.
visualDescription: A 1995 home computer screen showing an early auction listing with a bid counter ticking up, a vintage colorful eBay logo in the corner

quizQuestion: PayPal was founded in December 1998 — but under what original name, founded by Peter Thiel and Max Levchin?
explanation: PayPal was originally founded as Confinity by Peter Thiel, Max Levchin, and others in December 1998. It later merged with Elon Musk's X.com in 2000 and was renamed PayPal. The company was acquired by eBay in 2002 for $1.5 billion. Former PayPal employees went on to found YouTube, LinkedIn, Yelp, and other major companies.
visualDescription: A late-1990s startup office with the word 'Confinity' on the window, a whiteboard sketching a digital wallet concept

quizQuestion: Netflix was founded in 1997 as a DVD-by-mail service. In what year did it launch streaming, launching the era of binge-watching?
explanation: Netflix launched its video streaming service in 2007, a decade after founding as a DVD-by-mail service. The streaming launch eventually upended the cable TV industry and prompted major Hollywood studios to launch their own competing services in the late 2010s.
visualDescription: A split image: left side showing Netflix DVD mailers and a laptop in 2007, right side a modern 4K TV streaming interface with the red Netflix logo

quizQuestion: Which two co-founders launched Netflix in 1997 as a DVD-by-mail rental service that charged late fees — unlike Blockbuster?
explanation: Reed Hastings and Marc Randolph co-founded Netflix in 1997. Legend says Hastings was inspired after paying a $40 late fee for a VHS copy of Apollo 13 at Blockbuster — though Hastings has said this story may be embellished. Netflix disrupted and ultimately killed the video rental store industry.
visualDescription: A 1997 red envelope Netflix DVD mailer beside a blockbuster video store, a receipt showing a late fee circled in red

quizQuestion: In what year did Amazon Web Services launch its cloud computing infrastructure services, pioneering the modern cloud industry?
explanation: Amazon Web Services launched its cloud computing services in March 2006 with services like S3 (storage) and EC2 (computing). This allowed businesses to rent computing resources on demand instead of buying servers. AWS now generates more profit for Amazon than its retail operations.
visualDescription: A server room with glowing racks of equipment transforming into floating data clouds, the AWS orange logo hovering above

quizQuestion: Usenet, the internet's original discussion forum system conceived in 1979, was created by which two Duke University graduate students?
explanation: Tom Truscott and Jim Ellis conceived Usenet in 1979 at Duke University as a way to share news and messages between computer users. Established in 1980, Usenet predated the World Wide Web by over a decade and established the concept of topic-based online communities that later became forums and subreddits.
visualDescription: A 1980 university terminal showing a scrolling list of newsgroup topics, a student adding a reply to an early discussion thread

quizQuestion: Which social network, founded in 2003 and famous for letting users customize their profile pages with HTML, was the world's most visited website from 2005 to 2008 — before being dethroned by Facebook?
explanation: Myspace, launched August 1, 2003, by Chris DeWolfe and Tom Anderson, was the dominant social network for several years. It was particularly influential in music discovery. News Corp bought it for $580 million in 2005 — one of the most expensive failed acquisitions in tech history. Justin Timberlake later became part-owner.
visualDescription: A 2006 Myspace profile page with a custom background, an embedded music player, and a glittery animated GIF — peak mid-2000s internet aesthetic
